text:
When I was asked to reprint my article, “The Third International and the Struggle for National Liberation in South Africa,” my first instinct was to refuse. I wrote the piece at twenty-three for a graduate seminar at UCLA, and like many early forays into research, it relied on a limited selection of primary sources. At the time, I had set my sights on a daunting and ultimately impractical dissertation comparing the Black Left in South Africa and the U.S. South—an endeavor made impossible by the fact that I couldn't actually enter South Africa. In 1985, President P. W. Botha had declared a state of emergency, and my active role in protests at the South African Consulate in Beverly Hills essentially guaranteed my visa would be denied. 

Revisiting the essay three decades later, parts of it are undeniably cringeworthy, particularly the rigid Marxist jargon and the archival gaps. In 1986, after all, the Soviet Union had not yet collapsed, and its archives remained firmly shut. Yet, despite the limitations of my youth and the available resources, I still find sections of the work I am proud of. Drawing on Cedric Robinson’s then-recent *Black Marxism*, I argued that a Black radical tradition—built on African redemption, rural resistance to the 1913 Land Act, and burgeoning nationalism—had already established a vision for self-determination long before the Communist Party of South Africa (CPSA) arrived on the scene. 

In those early years, the CPSA was focused almost entirely on the white working class—famously backing strikes under the baffling slogan, “Workers of the World Unite and Fight for a WHITE SOUTH AFRICA!” It was only when African trade unionists and Comintern officials in Moscow challenged this myopia, specifically criticizing the Party’s obsession with the 1922 Rand Revolt over the struggles of African miners, that the direction began to shift. Looking back, the essay's core contribution was its attempt to flip the standard narrative: it wasn't just that Communists "infiltrated" the nationalist movement, but rather that African nationalists fundamentally transformed the Communist Party, forcing a roadmap for Black majority rule and the return of stolen land.
